RQ1,RQ1.txt::chunk2,". Architecturally, the EST service is located between a Certification Authority (CA) and a client. It performs several functions traditionally allocated to the Registration Authority (RA) role in a PKI. The nature of communication between an EST server and a CA is not described in this document. EST adopts the Certificate Management Protocol (CMP) [RFC4210] model for CA certificate rollover, but it does not use the CMP message syntax or protocol",us1005.c,partially_verified,partial,True,Shows both enrolled cert and CA cert retrieval from client context supporting EST's client-facing functionality.

RQ1,RQ1.txt::chunk1,"This document profiles certificate enrollment for clients using Certificate Management over CMS (CMC) [RFC5272] messages over a secure transport. Enrollment over Secure Transport (EST) describes the use of Transport Layer Security (TLS) 1.1 [RFC4346], 1.2 [RFC5246], or any future version) and Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) [RFC2616] to provide an authenticated and authorized channel for Simple Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) Requests and Responses [RFC5272]",us1159.c,partially_verified,partial,True,"Asserts successful authenticated enroll via est_client_enroll_csr, demonstrating client enrollment and HTTP auth setup but does not assert TLS version negotiation or CMC message usage."

RQ16,RQ16.txt::chunk2,The media types specified in the HTTP Content-Type header indicate which EST message is being transferred. Media types used by EST are specified in Section 3.2.4.,NO_TEST_CASE,no_assertion_evidence,weak,True,"The source code contains explicit logic that checks the 'Content-Type' header and compares the header value against an expected media type mapping (est_client_http.c), and the proxy/server code checks presence and errors on missing Content-Type. However, none of the provided test chunks contain an assertion that explicitly verifies that the Content-Type header is used to determine which EST message is being transferred (i.e., no test asserts that a particular Content-Type resulted in selection/dispatch to a specific EST operation, nor that incorrect/missing Content-Type yields EST_ERR_BAD_CONTENT_TYPE). Therefore there is an implementation-to-requirement link in code, but a lack of focused V&V tests that assert the required behavior and cover positive, negative, and boundary cases listed in Section 3.2.4."

RQ16,RQ16.txt::chunk3,HTTP redirections (3xx status codes) to the same web origin (see [RFC6454]) SHOULD be handled by the client without user input so long as all applicable security checks (Sections 3.3 and 3.6) have been enforced on the initial connection. The client initiates a new TLS connection and performs all applicable security checks when redirected to other web origin servers,NO_TEST_CASE,no_assertion_evidence,weak,True,"No candidate tests or assertions were provided to verify RQ16. The supplied client-side code (est_client_http.c) contains a switch over http_status for 200/202/204/400/401/404 and default cases but contains no explicit handling of 3xx redirect codes or logic to compare origins and decide whether to auto-follow or re-initiate TLS. Server-side helpers (should_keep_alive, est_server.c) reference connection lifecycle and specific status codes (202, 4xx) but do not implement or demonstrate the client redirect/origin/TLS behaviors required by RQ16."

RQ16,RQ16.txt::chunk4,". Redirections to other web origins require the EST client to obtain user input for non-GET or HEAD requests as specified in [RFC2616]. Additionally, if the client has already generated a CSR that includes linking identity and POP information (Section 3.5), then the CSR will need to be recreated to incorporate the tls-unique from the new, redirected session. Note: the key pair need not be regenerated. These are processing and interface burdens on the client",NO_TEST_CASE,no_assertion_evidence,weak,True,"No candidate test contains assertions that (a) detect or handle HTTP 3xx redirects to a different web origin with explicit user confirmation for non-GET/HEAD methods, or (b) demonstrate CSR re-creation using the existing keypair to embed a new tls-unique after a redirect that yields a new TLS session. Supporting code (est_client_enroll_pkcs10) contains logic to embed tls-unique into a CSR but does not implement redirection detection, user prompt on cross-origin redirects, or automatic CSR re-creation; therefore the provided test suite and artifacts do not demonstrate compliance with the requirement's redirection and user-interaction obligations."

Use of a PIN or one-time password can help mitigate such exposure, but it is RECOMMENDED that EST clients use such credentials only once to obtain a client certificate (that will be used during future interactions with the EST server).",us898.c,partially_verified,partial,True,"Asserts server returns 401 when HTTP auth credentials missing, evidencing Basic auth enforcement but not plaintext exposure or one-time credential use."

RQ51,RQ51.txt::chunk12,"As described in CMC, Section 6.7 of [RFC5272], ""For keys that can be used as signature keys, signing the certification request with the private key serves as a POP on that key pair"". The inclusion of tls- unique within the certification request links the proof-of-possession to the TLS proof-of-identity by enforcing that the POP operation occurred while the TLS session was active. This implies to the server that the authenticated client currently has access to the private key",us899.c,partially_verified,partial,True,"Uses enroll_csr API with a private key and asserts enrollment success (implying PoP path), but does not assert that the CSR contained the tls-unique value or that the server compared it to the TLS UID."

RQ51,RQ51.txt::chunk17,"ASN.1 encoding rules (e.g., DER and BER) have a type-length-value structure, and it is easy to construct malicious content with invalid length fields that can cause buffer overrun conditions. ASN.1 encoding rules allow for arbitrary levels of nesting, which may make it possible to construct malicious content that will cause a stack overflow",us899.c,partially_verified,partial,True,Directly asserts oversized/invalid input is rejected (csr == NULL) but does not exercise crafted TLV length encodings or nesting-depth attacks.

RQ51,RQ51.txt::chunk4,. It is RECOMMENDED that the Implicit Trust Anchor database used for EST server authentication be carefully managed to reduce the chance of a third-party CA with poor certification practices from being trusted. Disabling the Implicit Trust Anchor database after successfully receiving the Distribution of CA certificates response (Section 4.1.3) limits any vulnerability to the first TLS exchange.,us900.c,partially_verified,partial,True,Asserts successful GET /cacerts and copying of returned CA certs but contains no assertion that the implicit trust anchor database is disabled thereafter.

RQ51,RQ51.txt::chunk16,"Regarding the CSR attributes that the CA may list for inclusion in an enrollment request, there are no real inherent security issues with the content being conveyed, but an adversary who is able to interpose herself into the conversation could exclude attributes that a server may want, include attributes that a server may not want, and render meaningless other attributes that a server may want.",us900.c,partially_verified,partial,True,Asserting client observes no attributes models exclusion-of-attributes outcome but simulates server-side absence rather than adversarial in‑transit removal.

RQ51,RQ51.txt::chunk17,"ASN.1 encoding rules (e.g., DER and BER) have a type-length-value structure, and it is easy to construct malicious content with invalid length fields that can cause buffer overrun conditions. ASN.1 encoding rules allow for arbitrary levels of nesting, which may make it possible to construct malicious content that will cause a stack overflow",us900.c,partially_verified,partial,True,"Asserts parameter/length checks reject zero or excessively large CSR attribute lengths, mitigating naive oversized inputs but not internal TLV length manipulations or nesting depth exploits."
